label: Riyadh — Capital
sub: Capital city and seat of the Saudi royal court since 1824. Population ~7.5 million. Center of government, military command, and Vision 2030 project coordination.

label: Mecca — Holiest City
sub: Holiest city in Islam; home of the Grand Mosque (Masjid al-Haram) and the Kaaba. Non-Muslims prohibited. Site of the 1979 Grand Mosque seizure by Juhayman al-Otaybi. ~2.5 million pilgrims perform Hajj annually.

label: Medina — Second Holy City
sub: Islam's second holiest city. Home of Masjid al-Nabawi (Prophet's Mosque) and the burial site of Muhammad. Captured by Ibn Saud's forces in December 1924.

label: Jeddah — Commercial Capital
sub: Saudi Arabia's largest Red Sea port and commercial hub. Major diplomatic center hosting many foreign embassies. Gateway city for Hajj pilgrims. Site of Saudi-Iran rapprochement talks (2023).

label: Dir'iyah — Original Saudi Capital
sub: Site of the 1744 Wahhabi-Saudi alliance that founded the First Saudi State. Razed by Egyptian-Ottoman forces in 1818. Now a UNESCO World Heritage Site (At-Turaif District, 2010) and major Vision 2030 heritage tourism development.

label: Taif — Summer Capital
sub: Mountain city east of Mecca, traditional summer residence of Saudi royalty and diplomatic corps. Site of September 1924 battle where Ibn Saud's forces defeated Hashemite defenders. Known for rose oil production.

label: Ha'il — Former Rashidi Capital
sub: Capital of the rival Rashidi dynasty that defeated and exiled the Al Saud family in 1891. The Rashidis' defeat of the Second Saudi State forced Ibn Saud's family into exile in Kuwait. Fell to Ibn Saud's forces in 1921.

label: Buraidah — Religious Heartland
sub: Capital of Al-Qassim province, considered the most religiously conservative region of Saudi Arabia. Historically resistant to modernization; a bastion of Wahhabi clerical influence. Population ~600,000.

label: Dhahran — Aramco HQ & Oil Capital
sub: Headquarters of Saudi Aramco, the world's largest oil company. Near the site of Dammam Well No. 7, where commercial oil was discovered on March 3, 1938. Hub of Saudi Arabia's Eastern Province oil industry.

label: Abqaiq — World's Largest Oil Plant
sub: World's largest crude oil processing and stabilization facility, processing up to 7 million barrels per day. Attacked by drones and cruise missiles on September 14, 2019, disrupting ~5.7M bbl/day. Attributed by US and allies to Iran.

label: Ras Tanura — Key Export Terminal
sub: One of the world's largest oil export terminals on the Persian Gulf, handling a major share of Saudi Aramco's crude exports. Critical node in global energy supply chains. Operated by Aramco since the 1940s.

label: Ghawar — World's Largest Oil Field
sub: Ghawar is the world's largest conventional oil field, approximately 280 km long and 30 km wide in Saudi Arabia's Eastern Province. Discovered in 1948, it has produced more than 60 billion barrels and has a current production capacity exceeding 3.8 million bpd.

label: Yanbu — Red Sea Industrial Port
sub: Major industrial city and oil export terminal on the Red Sea, connected to Eastern Province oil fields via pipeline. Part of Saudi Arabia's industrial corridor established under King Fahd's development plans of the 1970s–1980s.

label: Jubail — Industrial City
sub: One of the largest industrial cities in the world, developed by the Royal Commission for Jubail and Yanbu. Hosts petrochemical, steel, and energy companies. Established in 1975 as part of Saudi Arabia's industrialization drive.

label: Shaybah — Remote Super-Giant Field
sub: Remote super-giant oil and gas field deep in the Empty Quarter (Rub' al Khali) desert. Reached by a 258 km paved road through the desert. Produces ~1 million bpd of Arabian Extra Light crude and is a key future growth asset for Aramco.

label: NEOM / The Line
sub: MBS's futuristic $500B+ megacity project in Tabuk Province. 'The Line' — a 170 km linear city — was scaled back to a 5 km segment by 2030. Construction suspended in September 2025. Forced displacement of the Howeitat tribe drew international condemnation.

label: Al-Ula — Heritage Tourism Site
sub: Ancient city in northwest Saudi Arabia containing Hegra (Mada'in Salih), a UNESCO World Heritage Site of Nabataean architecture. Saudi Arabia's first UNESCO site (2008). Under major Vision 2030 heritage tourism development led by the Royal Commission for Al Ula.

label: KAUST — Science & Technology University
sub: King Abdullah University of Science and Technology, opened September 23, 2009 near Thuwal on the Red Sea. Saudi Arabia's first co-educational university and its top research institution. Endowed with $20 billion by King Abdullah as a 'gift to the world.'

label: Amaala — Red Sea Luxury Tourism
sub: Ultra-luxury tourism development on the Red Sea coast near NEOM. One of Saudi Arabia's flagship Vision 2030 tourism projects, featuring yacht clubs, private beaches, and wellness facilities. Part of the broader Red Sea Project initiative.

label: KAEC — King Abdullah Economic City
sub: King Abdullah Economic City north of Jeddah — a $100B planned city announced in 2005. Significantly scaled back from original ambitions but houses the King Abdullah Port, one of Saudi Arabia's busiest commercial ports, and a growing industrial zone.

label: Riyadh — Diplomatic Quarter
sub: Host of over 100 foreign embassies. Hub of Saudi diplomatic activity. Site of the 2023 Arab League Jeddah Summit that restored Syrian membership. Many Western governments maintain large diplomatic missions here, central to US-Saudi alliance management.

label: King Fahd Causeway — Bahrain Link
sub: 24 km causeway linking Saudi Arabia to Bahrain, opened November 25, 1986. Critical transit route for Saudi troops deployed to Bahrain during the 2011 Arab Spring to support the Al-Khalifa monarchy. Symbol of Saudi dominance over Gulf security.

label: Khobar Towers — 1996 Bombing Site
sub: Site of the June 25, 1996 truck bombing of US Air Force housing that killed 19 American personnel and wounded 372. The FBI implicated Saudi Hezbollah with Iranian direction. The attack triggered major US force realignment away from Saudi Arabia.

label: Ritz-Carlton Riyadh — 2017 Purge Site
sub: Site of MBS's November 4, 2017 anti-corruption purge, where 200+ princes, ministers, and businessmen were detained. The Saudi government announced $107 billion in 'recovered' assets. Human rights groups reported coercion and physical mistreatment of detainees.

label: Najran — Saudi-Yemen Border
sub: Saudi city near the Yemen border that has been a target of Houthi cross-border attacks since the start of the Yemen war in 2015, including ballistic missiles and drone strikes. Also home to a significant Shia minority (Ismaili community), historically marginalized.
